text: The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram during a robotic-assisted single incision cholecystectomy, visualizing the bile duct anatomy.
The surgeon performs piecemeal dissection of the triangle of Calot to achieve visualization of the critical view, carefully separating anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon alternates dissection between the anterior and posterior views to manage moderate inflammation during the robotic-assisted single incision cholecystectomy.
The surgeon dissects approximately one third of the distance of the gallbladder fossa, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomy before clipping or cutting any structures.

At the start of the operation, a cholangiogram catheter is inserted into the abdomen through a fourteen French angiocatheter placed in the epigastric region, visible as a small needle puncture without requiring an incision.
The surgeon places one clip on the distal cystic duct and two clips on the cystic artery after obtaining the critical view of safety during the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to perform a ductotomy on the cystic duct, creating an incision in the duct wall.
The surgeon places the third arterial clip and uses the empty clip applier to maneuver the cholangiogram catheter into the cystic duct.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram by undocking one robotic arm. Following this, clips are placed on the proximal cystic duct.
The surgeon transects the cystic duct and cystic artery, then removes the gallbladder from the fossa using electrocautery.
The surgeon closes the abdominal wall using standard suturing techniques, and the angiocatheter puncture site is sealed with Dermabond or Steri-Strips.
